principle: Use a consistent type scale with a defined ratio between sizes.
rationale: A mathematical scale creates visual hierarchy that feels intentional and harmonious — the eye perceives proportional relationships as natural order.
example: Do: 14px, 16px, 20px, 25px, 31px (1.25 ratio). Don't: arbitrary jumps like 14px, 18px, 22px, 30px.

principle: Limit to 3-4 distinct typographic levels per page or section.
rationale: More than four levels dilutes hierarchy — the user's eye stops knowing where to look first, and every level loses its meaning.
example: Do: Display, Heading, Body, Caption. Don't: H1 through H5 plus Body plus Small plus Tiny — eight levels compete with each other.

principle: Keep body text between 45-75 characters per line.
rationale: The eye's return sweep on long lines causes fatigue and increases the chance of re-reading the same line — 65 characters is the sweet spot for comfortable reading.
example: Do: max-width: 65ch on prose containers. Don't: full-width paragraphs spanning 1200px on a widescreen monitor.

principle: Pair a distinctive display font with a neutral body font. Maximum two families plus one mono.
rationale: Contrast between expressive display and restrained body creates hierarchy without friction — adding a third expressive family introduces noise, not richness.
example: Do: Syne (display) + Outfit (body) + IBM Plex Mono (code). Don't: five different fonts across headings, subheadings, body, captions, and labels.

principle: Body text needs 1.5-1.7 line-height. Display text needs 1.0-1.2.
rationale: Line-height should tighten as type size increases — large text at 1.6 looks floaty and disconnected, while small body text at 1.1 becomes unreadable.
example: Do: body { line-height: 1.6 } and h1 { line-height: 1.05 }. Don't: apply line-height: 1.5 to everything uniformly.

principle: Use an 8px base unit for all spacing.
rationale: An 8px grid aligns with device pixel densities and creates consistent visual rhythm — arbitrary spacing values produce subtle misalignments that make interfaces feel unpolished.
example: Do: 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64px. Don't: 10, 15, 23, 37px — values that don't relate to any base unit.

principle: Section gaps should be 2-3x larger than element gaps.
rationale: Gestalt proximity — elements close together are perceived as a group. If section gaps equal element gaps, the page reads as a single undifferentiated mass.
example: Do: card gap 16px, section gap 48-64px. Don't: 24px everywhere, making sections and cards indistinguishable.

principle: Cards need at least 24px padding, often 32-48px.
rationale: Cramped padding signals amateur design — generous padding communicates confidence and makes content feel premium and breathable.
example: Do: .card { padding: 32px }. Don't: padding: 12px, which squashes content against card edges.

principle: Whitespace directs attention. Use asymmetric whitespace intentionally.
rationale: Uneven space guides the eye — more space above creates separation, more space below creates association with the following element. Equal space on all sides stops directing the eye.
example: Do: 64px margin-top before a heading, 16px margin-bottom after it, linking it visually to its content. Don't: equal 32px on all sides.

principle: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast for body text and 3:1 for large text.
rationale: Insufficient contrast is both a legal liability and a readability failure — 1 in 12 users has some form of color vision deficiency, and low contrast affects everyone in bright environments.
example: Do: #e5e2dc on #08080c achieves 13.5:1. Don't: #666 on #333 at 2.8:1 which fails AA entirely.

principle: Apply the 60-30-10 rule: dominant background, secondary surface, accent.
rationale: Timid palettes that use accent colors sparingly feel generic — a bold 10% accent reads as intentional design, while 2% accent disappears into the background noise.
example: Do: 60% dark background, 30% elevated surface color, 10% gold accent across interactive elements. Don't: accent only on one button.

principle: The same color must mean the same thing everywhere in the interface.
rationale: Users build color associations within 30 seconds — if success is green on one screen and blue on another, the system feels broken and untrustworthy.
example: Do: use consistent --success, --error, --warning semantic tokens across every component. Don't: reuse accent color for both links and error states.

principle: Never use color as the only way to convey information.
rationale: Approximately 8% of men have color vision deficiency — using color alone to signal errors or status excludes a significant portion of users.
example: Do: red text + error icon + 'Error:' prefix label. Don't: just turning a border red to indicate a failed field.

principle: Every page needs one clear focal point — one element that demands attention first.
rationale: If everything is important, nothing is. Pages without a dominant element force users to scan randomly, increasing cognitive load and reducing comprehension.
example: Do: hero at 4rem, section headings at 2rem, body at 1rem — a clear size cascade. Don't: hero at 2.5rem and sections at 2rem, creating no dominant element.

principle: Align everything to the grid, then break it intentionally for emphasis.
rationale: Random misalignment reads as broken — but a deliberate grid break creates visual tension that draws the eye. The difference is intent and consistency.
example: Do: 12-column grid with one hero image that bleeds to the edge intentionally. Don't: elements randomly misaligned by 7px or 13px with no pattern.

principle: Match information density to the purpose of the interface.
rationale: Marketing pages that are too dense overwhelm visitors before they convert. Dashboards that are too sparse waste screen real estate and require excessive scrolling.
example: Do: landing page with 64-96px section gaps, dashboard with 12-16px gaps between data points. Don't: apply the same density to both.

principle: Design three distinct layouts — mobile, tablet, desktop — not one layout that shrinks.
rationale: Simply stacking columns is not responsive design — it ignores the different usage contexts, content priorities, and interaction patterns of each breakpoint.
example: Do: reorder elements on mobile, promote key CTAs higher, simplify navigation. Don't: just stack all columns vertically and call it responsive.

principle: Use named z-index layers with defined token values, never arbitrary numbers.
rationale: Arbitrary z-index values create a race to the top — the next developer adds z-index: 9999 to override the last, and the system collapses into chaos.
example: Do: --z-dropdown: 10, --z-sticky: 20, --z-modal: 30, --z-toast: 40. Don't: z-index: 9999 or z-index: 99999.

principle: Every animation must serve a purpose: orient, guide, confirm, or delight.
rationale: Gratuitous animation increases cognitive load and fatigues users — motion that serves no purpose trains users to ignore all motion, including the purposeful kind.
example: Do: staggered card reveal that orients the user to the page structure. Don't: every button wiggling on hover with no informational value.

principle: UI feedback: 150-300ms. Content reveals: 300-600ms. Never exceed 1 second.
rationale: Animations slower than 300ms for micro-interactions feel sluggish and make the interface feel unresponsive — longer durations are only appropriate for content entering the viewport.
example: Do: hover state 180ms, dropdown open 220ms, page section reveal 400ms. Don't: 800ms on a button hover or 2s on a modal open.

principle: Use ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits. Never use linear for UI animations.
rationale: Natural motion accelerates and decelerates — objects in the physical world don't move at constant speed. Linear motion reads as mechanical and unnatural.
example: Do: slide-in with 300ms ease-out, slide-out with 200ms ease-in. Don't: all transitions at 300ms linear.

principle: Always respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query.
rationale: Vestibular disorders affect approximately 35% of adults over 40 — motion that triggers vestibular responses is not a preference, it is a disability accommodation requirement.
example: Do: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { * { animation-duration: 0.01ms; transition-duration: 0.01ms; } }. Don't: ignore this query entirely.

principle: Every interactive element must have a clearly visible focus indicator.
rationale: Keyboard navigation is essential for users who cannot use a mouse — removing focus styles makes the interface completely unusable for this group.
example: Do: :focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; }. Don't: outline: none without a custom replacement.

principle: Interactive elements must have a minimum 44x44px touch target size.
rationale: Apple and Google both mandate 44px minimum touch targets — smaller targets cause mis-taps, frustrate users with motor difficulties, and fail platform accessibility guidelines.
example: Do: min-height: 44px; min-width: 44px on buttons and links. Don't: 24px icon buttons with no padding that require pixel-precise tapping.

principle: Use semantic HTML elements, maintain heading order, and include landmark regions.
rationale: Screen readers navigate by landmarks and headings — without semantic structure, blind users cannot jump to content or understand page organization.
example: Do: <nav>, <main>, <aside>, headings in order from <h1> through <h3>. Don't: div soup with no landmark roles or headings that jump from h1 to h4.

principle: Every form input needs a persistent visible label — placeholders are not labels.
rationale: Placeholders disappear when the user starts typing, leaving them unable to remember what a field is for — this particularly affects users with cognitive disabilities.
example: Do: <label for='email'>Email</label><input id='email' placeholder='you@example.com'>. Don't: <input placeholder='Email'> with no associated label element.
